Author: Pratham Sharma
Title: How to get most of your LLMs in development: Short guide to improving your vibe coding sessions
Text:
How to get most of your LLMs in development: Short guide to improving your vibe coding sessions
The engineering world ( and world in general ) currently is going through a interesting period which is industry breaking as well. A lot of engineering workforce is leaning towards skepticism or negativity. And major companies are leaning towards extreme optimism (any sam altman news article) and perhaps inevitablilism.
We decided to focus on productivity, very early on and have been early adapters of this new wave. One of the most breakthrough things, that I as engineer have experienced is the mass-code generation that is enabled through llms. We are generating almost all of our code through these llms as of now and surely there has been almost a next level of increase in productivity.
Just to put things in perspective, we decided to re-write our existing backend infra into micro service based system due to scaling reasons and due to current changes in LLMs and coding tools this seemed like THE best time to proceed with this. For this enormous task it would have taken easy 5–6 months of moving and debugging achieved but we have managed to move 80% of existing codebase to this in just a short span of a month! This is huge but our productivity isn’t only reliant on the llm’s spewing code.
Through lot of trial and testing, I have come across some key principles / ideas that we keep in mind as we do anything now-a-days. Following is my very amateur attempt to compile list of somewhat debatable and often adaptable strategies.
1. Orchestration is the key!
Create tasks, implementation PRDs, context files and establishing a sequential process between multiple agents. A solid strategy and plan only leads to good implementation if executed properly. So create as detailed orchestration strategy as possible. This has been a tried and tested method. Claude code and cursor also put out a very crucial feature of todo’s relevant to thinking models, few months ago only where you can see the model defining tasks and following those. Kiro code by amazon tried to execute this and honestly did a pretty amazing job by introducing concepts like hooks. Idea is to utilise as much resources in parallel or sequential in form of blocks as possible. These resources are at your disposable you have to connect the blocks only. One of this orchestration that I followed was a lot is Figma MCP has been with claude code to generate design screens in one go. Obviously we had to put a design system in place first to allow one-shotting through these screens but this MCP driven flow provide to be highly efficient. Another example is fixing linting errors and generating commit messages accordingly. So I registered a precommit hook which runs eslint on my codebase and then as soon the eslint fails a subagent is triggered in claude code that attempts to fix eslint errors. As codebases grow these minor orchestrations make great improvement.
2. Context is everything but also not
Without context LLMs cannot function. Generally approaching problems by defining a context dump is much more efficient than recreating the scenarios. I often want to copy a existing piece of code that may work in a codesandbox example but not on my architecture as it is not organized according to my architecture. In that scenario the codesandbox can be context to a broader feature. Similarly a big dump of payload cms storage adapters taken from context7 is extremely helpful when you want a straight example.
But more input tokens won’t always mean better result. More tokens can lead to context-rot which personally I have experienced a lot of times. Context rot was recently coined in research by chroma but basically lot of LLMs are trained and benchmarked on NIAH ( needle in a haystack ) problems. These benchmarks and problems focus on lexical matching rather than the testing flexible or task oriented performance. Solution to avoid context rot at user level is pretty simple. Keep the task in hand tunnel visioned. This increases the simplicity and model won’t have a hard time reasoning with the context provided. Even Andrej Kaparthy has conveyed heavily that as engineers we should be focusing more on context engineering rather than prompt engineering
3. LLMs are not interns (kinda)
One of the major misconception of using llms is treating them as interns or junior colleagues of sort. I have been adopting a mixed approach of treating them as a mindless instructor which logically suits their styles and attempt. This idea initially was given to me by a brilliant write-up by hazel weakly (must read). This idea of using LLMs as a mindless instructor is much more apt as LLMs have much more information than we can ever have. Ask any question to it based on any context and it will probably have a solution for you. This really helps in getting out of blockers in very early stages and thus makes you consume much lesser time.
For example a bug related to your s3 bucket configuration may take a day for you to solve. It may involve trying and testing various bucket policy configuration, it may involve changing sdk version, hell sometimes updating the code. This whole flow is so much streamlined as you can explore various solution on the fly
4. Think Further!
As the LLMs have evolved we are able to achieve objectives in short span of time. We are also able to achieve much more in terms of quantity. Planning ahead becomes much more crucial in modern workflows. I keep a mental model of simple 5 step ahead plan. Supposedly I want to create upload image feature for my social media application. So my mental model switches to:
- Uploaded images may need to have tagging feature as well
- Uploaded images may need to go through a compression layer service like imgproxy. Let’s find alternatives or spin up my own to test
- User should be able to have shareable links to images
- If user is deleted then images can be deleted as well Above example is a very simple one and you can imagine this any of your implementation goal.
Basically planning pros, cons, connections, alternatives and other items that you would have usually avoided before finishing the task in hand.
5. Monitor the LLMs by re-prompting
One of the biggest mistakes users make with LLMs is treating the first response as final. Unlike traditional software, LLMs are probabilistic — they generate outputs based on context and phrasing of your query. That means you can often get significantly better results simply by re-prompting. Idea is to not only get a better result by re-prompting but also understanding what style of language or framing works better. This is crucial for prompt engineering as well. Prompt engineering is all about structuring prompt well and clarifying the instructions. Some more key takeaways on re-prompting that I would like to add is Prompt structring and length. A super detailed prompt can often be a waste of time most of the better reasoning models can connect the dots themselves so sometimes you don’t have to be super detailed if you a have good (engineered) context and a very straight framed prompt. For example: Instead of saying
Write Python code using requests and BeautifulSoup to scrape article titles from https://news.ycombinator.com/
You can say
Write Python code using requests and BeautifulSoup to scrape article titles from https://news.ycombinator.com/
Requirements:
1. Handle pagination for multiple pages.
2. Include error handling for network issues.
3. Print the collected titles as a clean list.
6. Do not trust anything, Keep the flow as closed as possible
Constant testing can be annoying but is the necessary evil. If the loop of development is restrictive then the output is going to be better. Obviously there is fine line between restrictive and unnecessarily restrictive. Nonetheless constant testing both automated and manual will ensure code is upto the mark. Personally I often try to get into the core services and see how code is structured and if I need to tweak more items in rules or not, or whether a better prompt can fix this or not. Often times this helps me find loose ends and makes me use a better prompt or context for next use.
7. You will have to edit the code sometimes
This may change over the time but as soon as your codebase is going out of the LLMs context window, you will have issues that aren’t often connected to each other at all. Sometimes you might have to make a trade-off for speed in some smaller changes that are generally a find and replace. This idea of editing code should be done only when super necessary as the flows we are relying on tend to restrict the manual intervention but in one way or another a config change or file update is still required until the reasoning time for LLMs catastrophically decreases.
Conclusion
Again LLMs are a field of constant research and hence it duty of engineers to come through with system in all this chaos. To come to a conclusion and approach of engineering in this current evolving craft. It is highly possible that above mentioned may work totally opposite for you. Nonetheless any software engineering pipeline was not achieved to their current standing in a single day but with constant efforts of industry as whole. So keep sharing what you find.
